text.title: Build vs Buy Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every decision-tree-build-vs-buy application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Any build vs buy decision with a 3-year TCO exceeding $50k MUST include a formal TCO comparison. The TCO MUST cover: initial development/licensing, 3-year maintenance/subscription, integration effort, team training, and opportunity cost of engineering time diverted from core product.
rule.rationale: Most build decisions undercount maintenance cost (typically 15-20% of build cost per year), security audit costs, and the ongoing engineering opportunity cost. Formal TCO analysis prevents these underestimates from driving incorrect decisions.

rule.statement: Decision MUST score all four axes (strategic differentiation, 3-yr TCO, time-to-value, lock-in risk). Partial scoring is blocked.
rule.rationale: Skipping an axis is the most common path to a regretted choice; the rule forces full evaluation.

rule.statement: Build is chosen ONLY when strategic_differentiation scores ≥4 on the 1-5 scale. Below 4 the default is buy or adopt-oss.

rationale.source: Marc Andreessen - 'companies should only build what differentiates'

rule.rationale: Below-threshold differentiation means the work creates no competitive moat; building it consumes capacity that should fund the actual moat.

rule.statement: Buy decisions MUST include a 3-year TCO model with realistic seat / API-call growth.
rule.rationale: Year-1 sticker price is misleading; tier transitions in year 2-3 frequently double the cost.

rule.statement: Buy decisions with lock_in_risk = high MUST include a rollback path with engineering_weeks estimate and trigger conditions.
rule.rationale: Without a rollback path the buy is implicitly one_way_door_irrevocable; the rule surfaces the actual reversibility tier.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
